texts.orig: Programming Guidelines

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thus, execution time is only a small part of the overall cost equation.You must balance execution time considerations with design quality, standards, and  customer  requirements. In particular, do not sacrifice clarity and correctness for speed.
texts.text: Thus, execution time is only a small part of the overall cost equation.You must balance execution time considerations with design quality, standards, and  customer  requirements. In particular, do not sacrifice clarity and correctness for speed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If speed is important to your implementation, you must learn how your compiler optimizes your code. Otherwise, the optimization may take your seemingly faster code and actually slow it down. To see how this paradoxical situation can happen, suppose you are writing code to implement a three-dimensional array. You decide to increase efficiency by creating instead a one-dimensional array and performing all the indexing computations yourself.Thus, your code computes such variables as
texts.text: If speed is important to your implementation, you must learn how your compiler optimizes your code. Otherwise, the optimization may take your seemingly faster code and actually slow it down. To see how this paradoxical situation can happen, suppose you are writing code to implement a three-dimensional array. You decide to increase efficiency by creating instead a one-dimensional array and performing all the indexing computations yourself.Thus, your code computes such variables as

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: index = 3*i + 2*j + k;

texts.label: text
texts.orig: to calculate the position of an entry in a three-dimensional array. However, your compiler may perform its array indexing in the registers, so execution time is small. If the compiler uses an additive increment technique in the registers, rather than adding and multiplying for each position calculation, then your one-dimensional array technique may actually result in increased execution time!
texts.text: to calculate the position of an entry in a three-dimensional array. However, your compiler may perform its array indexing in the registers, so execution time is small. If the compiler uses an additive increment technique in the registers, rather than adding and multiplying for each position calculation, then your one-dimensional array technique may actually result in increased execution time!

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Data Structures
texts.text: Data Structures

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In writing your programs, you should format and store data so that data management and manipulation are straightforward. There are several techniques that use the structure of the data to suggest how the program should be organized.
texts.text: In writing your programs, you should format and store data so that data management and manipulation are straightforward. There are several techniques that use the structure of the data to suggest how the program should be organized.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Keeping the Program Simple. The program's design may specify some of the data structures to be used in implementing functions. Often, these structures are chosen because they fit into an overall scheme that promotes information hiding and control of component  interfaces. Data  manipulation  within  a  component  can  influence  your choice of data structures in a similar way. For example, restructuring data can simplify a program's calculations.To see how, suppose you are writing a program to determine the amount  of  federal  income  tax  due. As  input, you  are  given  the  amount  of  taxable income and are told the following:
texts.text: Keeping the Program Simple. The program's design may specify some of the data structures to be used in implementing functions. Often, these structures are chosen because they fit into an overall scheme that promotes information hiding and control of component  interfaces. Data  manipulation  within  a  component  can  influence  your choice of data structures in a similar way. For example, restructuring data can simplify a program's calculations.To see how, suppose you are writing a program to determine the amount  of  federal  income  tax  due. As  input, you  are  given  the  amount  of  taxable income and are told the following:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. For the first $10,000 of income, the tax is 10%.
texts.text: For the first $10,000 of income, the tax is 10%.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. For the next $10,000 of income above $10,000, the tax is 12%.
texts.text: For the next $10,000 of income above $10,000, the tax is 12%.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. For the next $10,000 of income above $20,000, the tax is 15%.
texts.text: For the next $10,000 of income above $20,000, the tax is 15%.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. For the next $10,000 of income above $30,000, the tax is 18%.
texts.text: For the next $10,000 of income above $30,000, the tax is 18%.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 5. For any income above $40,000, the tax is 20%.
texts.text: For any income above $40,000, the tax is 20%.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thus, someone who has a taxable income of $35,000 pays 10% of the first $10,000 (or $1000), 12% of the next $10,000 (or $1200), 15% of the next $10,000 (or $1500), and 18% of the remaining $5000 (or $900), for a total of $4600. To calculate the tax, you can include code in your component that reads in the taxable income and follows this algorithm:
texts.text: Thus, someone who has a taxable income of $35,000 pays 10% of the first $10,000 (or $1000), 12% of the next $10,000 (or $1200), 15% of the next $10,000 (or $1500), and 18% of the remaining $5000 (or $900), for a total of $4600. To calculate the tax, you can include code in your component that reads in the taxable income and follows this algorithm:

texts.label: code
texts.orig: tax = 0. if (taxable_income == 0) goto EXIT; if (taxable_income > 10000) tax = tax + 1000;
texts.text: tax = 0. if (taxable_income == 0) goto EXIT; if (taxable_income > 10000) tax = tax + 1000;

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, we can define a tax table  for  each 'bracket' of  tax  liability, as  shown  in Table 7.1, where we use a base figure and a percentage for each bracket.
texts.text: However, we can define a tax table  for  each 'bracket' of  tax  liability, as  shown  in Table 7.1, where we use a base figure and a percentage for each bracket.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Then, using the table, we can simplify our algorithm considerably:
texts.text: Then, using the table, we can simplify our algorithm considerably:

texts.label: code
texts.orig: for (int i-2; level=1; i <= 5; i++) if (taxable_income > bracket[i]) level = level + 1; tax = base[level]+percent[level]*(taxable_income-bracket[level]);
texts.text: for (int i-2; level=1; i <= 5; i++) if (taxable_income > bracket[i]) level = level + 1; tax = base[level]+percent[level]*(taxable_income-bracket[level]);

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Notice  how  the  calculations  are  simplified  just  by  changing  the  way  the  data  are defined.This simplification makes the program easier to understand, test, and modify.
texts.text: Notice  how  the  calculations  are  simplified  just  by  changing  the  way  the  data  are defined.This simplification makes the program easier to understand, test, and modify.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Using  a  Data  Structure  to  Determine  a  Program  Structure. In  the  tax  table example, the way we define the data dictates how we perform the necessary calculations. In general, data structures can influence the organization and flow of a program. In  some  cases, the  data  structures  can  influence  the  choice  of  language, too. For

texts.label: text
texts.orig: example, LISP is designed to be a list processor, and it contains structures that make it much more attractive than some other languages for handling lists. Similarly, Ada and Eiffel contain constructs for handling unacceptable states called exceptions.
texts.text: example, LISP is designed to be a list processor, and it contains structures that make it much more attractive than some other languages for handling lists. Similarly, Ada and Eiffel contain constructs for handling unacceptable states called exceptions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A data structure is said to be recursive if it is defined by identifying an initial element  and  then  generating  successive  elements  as  a  function  of  those  previously defined. For example, a rooted tree is a graph composed of nodes and lines so that the following conditions hold:
texts.text: A data structure is said to be recursive if it is defined by identifying an initial element  and  then  generating  successive  elements  as  a  function  of  those  previously defined. For example, a rooted tree is a graph composed of nodes and lines so that the following conditions hold:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Exactly one node of the tree is designated as the root.
texts.text: Exactly one node of the tree is designated as the root.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. If the lines emanating from the root are erased, the resulting graph is a set of nonintersecting graphs, each of which is a rooted tree.
texts.text: If the lines emanating from the root are erased, the resulting graph is a set of nonintersecting graphs, each of which is a rooted tree.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 7.1 illustrates a rooted tree, and Figure 7.2 shows how removing the root results in a set of smaller rooted trees.The root of each smaller tree is the node that had previously been connected to the original root of the larger tree.Thus, the rooted tree is defined in terms of its root and subtrees: a recursive definition.
texts.text: Figure 7.1 illustrates a rooted tree, and Figure 7.2 shows how removing the root results in a set of smaller rooted trees.The root of each smaller tree is the node that had previously been connected to the original root of the larger tree.Thus, the rooted tree is defined in terms of its root and subtrees: a recursive definition.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Programming languages such as Pascal allow recursive procedures to deal with recursive data structures. You may prefer to use recursive procedures for this type of data, since their use forces the burden of managing the data structure to be borne by the compiler rather than your program.The use of recursion may make the actual programming easier or may make your program more understandable. Thus, in general, you should consider the data structures carefully when deciding which language to use in implementing the design.
texts.text: Programming languages such as Pascal allow recursive procedures to deal with recursive data structures. You may prefer to use recursive procedures for this type of data, since their use forces the burden of managing the data structure to be borne by the compiler rather than your program.The use of recursion may make the actual programming easier or may make your program more understandable. Thus, in general, you should consider the data structures carefully when deciding which language to use in implementing the design.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: General Guidelines
texts.text: General Guidelines

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several overall strategies are useful in preserving the design quality in your code.
texts.text: Several overall strategies are useful in preserving the design quality in your code.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Localizing Input and Output. Those parts of a program that read input or generate output are highly specialized and must reflect characteristics of the underlying hardware and software. Because of this dependence, the program sections performing input and output functions are sometimes difficult to test. In fact, they may be the sections  most  likely  to  change  if  the  hardware  or  software  is  modified. Therefore, it  is desirable to localize these sections in components separate from the rest of the code.
texts.text: Localizing Input and Output. Those parts of a program that read input or generate output are highly specialized and must reflect characteristics of the underlying hardware and software. Because of this dependence, the program sections performing input and output functions are sometimes difficult to test. In fact, they may be the sections  most  likely  to  change  if  the  hardware  or  software  is  modified. Therefore, it  is desirable to localize these sections in components separate from the rest of the code.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An added benefit of localization is generalization of the overall system. Other systemwide  functions  to  be  performed  on  the  input  (such  as  reformatting  or  type checking) can be included in the specialized component, relieving the other components of the burden and thus eliminating repetition. Similarly, putting output functions in one place makes the system easier to understand and change.
texts.text: An added benefit of localization is generalization of the overall system. Other systemwide  functions  to  be  performed  on  the  input  (such  as  reformatting  or  type checking) can be included in the specialized component, relieving the other components of the burden and thus eliminating repetition. Similarly, putting output functions in one place makes the system easier to understand and change.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Including Pseudocode. The design usually lays out a framework for each program  component. Then, you  add  your  creativity  and  expertise  to  build  the  lines  of code that implement the design. For example, the design may be relatively languageindependent, giving you many choices about the particular language constructs to use, how to use them, how the data will be represented, and so on. Since the design is an outline of what is to be done in a program component, it is useful to move in stages from the specified design to the code, rather than to translate the design immediately into code.
texts.text: Including Pseudocode. The design usually lays out a framework for each program  component. Then, you  add  your  creativity  and  expertise  to  build  the  lines  of code that implement the design. For example, the design may be relatively languageindependent, giving you many choices about the particular language constructs to use, how to use them, how the data will be represented, and so on. Since the design is an outline of what is to be done in a program component, it is useful to move in stages from the specified design to the code, rather than to translate the design immediately into code.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pseudocode can be used to adapt the design to your chosen language. By adopting constructs and data representations without becoming involved immediately in the specifics of each command, you can experiment and decide which implementation is most desirable. In this way, code can be rearranged and restructured with a minimum of rewriting. For instance, suppose the design for a component of a text processing system states

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thus, pseudocode has acted as a framework on which to construct the code. In briefing the  code  from  the  design  in  this  way, notice  that  the  design's  organization  has changed several times. Such changes must be reported to and approved by the designers, so that the links among requirements, design, and code can be documented and maintained.
texts.text: Thus, pseudocode has acted as a framework on which to construct the code. In briefing the  code  from  the  design  in  this  way, notice  that  the  design's  organization  has changed several times. Such changes must be reported to and approved by the designers, so that the links among requirements, design, and code can be documented and maintained.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Revising and Rewriting, Not Patching. When writing code, as when preparing a term paper or creating a work of art, you often write a rough draft. Then you carefully revise and rewrite until you are satisfied with the result. If you find the control flow convoluted, or the decision processes hard to understand, or the unconditional branches difficult to eliminate, then it may be time to return to the design. Reexamine the design to see whether the problems you are encountering are inherent in the design or in your translation to code. Look again at how the data are represented and structured, at the algorithms chosen, and at the decomposition.
texts.text: Revising and Rewriting, Not Patching. When writing code, as when preparing a term paper or creating a work of art, you often write a rough draft. Then you carefully revise and rewrite until you are satisfied with the result. If you find the control flow convoluted, or the decision processes hard to understand, or the unconditional branches difficult to eliminate, then it may be time to return to the design. Reexamine the design to see whether the problems you are encountering are inherent in the design or in your translation to code. Look again at how the data are represented and structured, at the algorithms chosen, and at the decomposition.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Reuse. There are two kinds of reuse: producer reuse , where we are creating components designed to be reused in subsequent applications, and consumer reuse , where  we  are  using  components  that  were  originally  developed  for  other  projects (Barnes and Bollinger 1991). Some companies have division-wide or company-wide reuse programs, with standards for evaluating and changing components; Sidebar 7.2 describes  such  a  program  at  Lucent. The  success  of  these  programs  suggests  reuse
texts.text: Reuse. There are two kinds of reuse: producer reuse , where we are creating components designed to be reused in subsequent applications, and consumer reuse , where  we  are  using  components  that  were  originally  developed  for  other  projects (Barnes and Bollinger 1991). Some companies have division-wide or company-wide reuse programs, with standards for evaluating and changing components; Sidebar 7.2 describes  such  a  program  at  Lucent. The  success  of  these  programs  suggests  reuse

texts.label: page_header

texts.text: Chapter 7

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Writing the Programs
texts.text: Writing the Programs

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: SIDEBAR 7.2 SELECTING COMPONENTS FOR REUSE AT LUCENT
texts.text: SIDEBAR 7.2 SELECTING COMPONENTS FOR REUSE AT LUCENT

texts.label: text
texts.orig: L ucent  Technologies  initiated  a  companywide  program  to  reuse  software  components (McClure 1997).As a consequence, the Workstation Software Development Department formed a Reuse Council to devise a strategy for selecting candidate components for its reuse repository.The Council consisted of seven people, representing all groups in the department. The Council created an inventory of components and formed a matrix with the features of all past  and  planned  projects. Then, each  feature  was  rated  in  terms  of  whether  it  had  been implemented and was still needed, had been implemented but was no longer needed, or had not been implemented but was still needed.Those features that were needed and were common to more than one project were targeted for reuse. In fact, some were redesigned to make them more reusable.
texts.text: L ucent  Technologies  initiated  a  companywide  program  to  reuse  software  components (McClure 1997).As a consequence, the Workstation Software Development Department formed a Reuse Council to devise a strategy for selecting candidate components for its reuse repository.The Council consisted of seven people, representing all groups in the department. The Council created an inventory of components and formed a matrix with the features of all past  and  planned  projects. Then, each  feature  was  rated  in  terms  of  whether  it  had  been implemented and was still needed, had been implemented but was no longer needed, or had not been implemented but was still needed.Those features that were needed and were common to more than one project were targeted for reuse. In fact, some were redesigned to make them more reusable.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  Council  met  every  week  for  two  hours  to  make  component  selections, inspect design documentation for those components already in the repository, and to monitor the levels of reuse in the department's projects.
texts.text: The  Council  met  every  week  for  two  hours  to  make  component  selections, inspect design documentation for those components already in the repository, and to monitor the levels of reuse in the department's projects.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: guidelines for you. If you are a consumer for your current project, there are four key characteristics to check about the components you are about to reuse:
texts.text: guidelines for you. If you are a consumer for your current project, there are four key characteristics to check about the components you are about to reuse:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Does the component perform the function or provide the data you need?
texts.text: Does the component perform the function or provide the data you need?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. If minor modification is required, is it less modification than building the component from scratch?
texts.text: If minor modification is required, is it less modification than building the component from scratch?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. Is the component well-documented, so you can understand it without having to verify its implementation line by line?
texts.text: Is the component well-documented, so you can understand it without having to verify its implementation line by line?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. Is there a complete record of the component's test and revision history, so you can be certain that it contains no faults?
texts.text: Is there a complete record of the component's test and revision history, so you can be certain that it contains no faults?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You must also assess the amount of code you need to write so that your system can interface with the reused components.
texts.text: You must also assess the amount of code you need to write so that your system can interface with the reused components.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: On the other hand, if you are a producer of reusable components, you should keep in mind several things:
texts.text: On the other hand, if you are a producer of reusable components, you should keep in mind several things:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Make your component general, using parameters and anticipating similar conditions to the ones in which your system will invoke your components.
texts.text: Make your component general, using parameters and anticipating similar conditions to the ones in which your system will invoke your components.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Separate dependencies so that sections likely to need change are isolated from those that are likely to remain the same.
texts.text: Separate dependencies so that sections likely to need change are isolated from those that are likely to remain the same.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Keep the component interface general and well-defined.
texts.text: Keep the component interface general and well-defined.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Include information about any faults found and fixed.
texts.text: Include information about any faults found and fixed.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Use clear naming conventions.
texts.text: Use clear naming conventions.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Document the data structures and algorithms.
texts.text: Document the data structures and algorithms.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Keep the communication and error-handling sections separate and easy to modify.
texts.text: Keep the communication and error-handling sections separate and easy to modify.

texts.text: Section 7.3

texts.text: Documentation

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 7.3 DOCUMENTATION
texts.text: 7.3 DOCUMENTATION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Many corporate or organizational standards and procedures focus on the descriptions accompanying a collection of programs.We consider program documentation to be the set of written descriptions that explain to a reader what the programs do and how they do it. Internal documentation is descriptive material written directly within the code; all other documentation is external documentation .
texts.text: Many corporate or organizational standards and procedures focus on the descriptions accompanying a collection of programs.We consider program documentation to be the set of written descriptions that explain to a reader what the programs do and how they do it. Internal documentation is descriptive material written directly within the code; all other documentation is external documentation .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Internal Documentation
texts.text: Internal Documentation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The internal  documentation  contains  information  directed  at  someone  who  will  be reading the source code of your programs. Thus, summary information is provided to identify the program and describe its data structures, algorithms, and control flow. Usually, this information is placed at the beginning of each component in a set of comments called the header comment block .
texts.text: The internal  documentation  contains  information  directed  at  someone  who  will  be reading the source code of your programs. Thus, summary information is provided to identify the program and describe its data structures, algorithms, and control flow. Usually, this information is placed at the beginning of each component in a set of comments called the header comment block .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Header Comment Block. Just as a good newspaper reporter includes the who, what, where, when, how, and why of a story, you must include the following information in your header comment block for each component:
texts.text: Header Comment Block. Just as a good newspaper reporter includes the who, what, where, when, how, and why of a story, you must include the following information in your header comment block for each component:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. what your component is called
texts.text: what your component is called

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. who wrote the component
texts.text: who wrote the component

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. where the component fits in the general system design
texts.text: where the component fits in the general system design

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. when the component was written and revised
texts.text: when the component was written and revised

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 5. why the component exists
texts.text: why the component exists

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 6. how the component uses its data structures, algorithms, and control
texts.text: how the component uses its data structures, algorithms, and control

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We can examine each of these pieces of information in more depth.
texts.text: We can examine each of these pieces of information in more depth.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: First, the name of the component must figure prominently in the documentation. Next, the block identifies the writer, with phone number or e-mail address, so that the maintenance and test teams can reach the writer with questions or comments.
texts.text: First, the name of the component must figure prominently in the documentation. Next, the block identifies the writer, with phone number or e-mail address, so that the maintenance and test teams can reach the writer with questions or comments.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: During the system's lifetime, components are often updated and revised, either for  fault  correction  or  because  requirements  change  and  grow. As  we  will  see  in Chapter 11, it is important to track the revisions, so program documentation should keep a log of changes made and who made them.
texts.text: During the system's lifetime, components are often updated and revised, either for  fault  correction  or  because  requirements  change  and  grow. As  we  will  see  in Chapter 11, it is important to track the revisions, so program documentation should keep a log of changes made and who made them.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Because the component is part of a larger system, the block should indicate how it fits in the component hierarchy. This information is sometimes conveyed with a diagram; at other times, a simple description will do.The header block should also explain how the component is invoked.
texts.text: Because the component is part of a larger system, the block should indicate how it fits in the component hierarchy. This information is sometimes conveyed with a diagram; at other times, a simple description will do.The header block should also explain how the component is invoked.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: More detailed  information  is  required  to  explain  how  the  component  accomplishes its goal.The block should list
texts.text: More detailed  information  is  required  to  explain  how  the  component  accomplishes its goal.The block should list

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · the name, type, and purpose of each major data structure and variable
texts.text: the name, type, and purpose of each major data structure and variable

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · brief descriptions of the logic flow, algorithms, and error handling
texts.text: brief descriptions of the logic flow, algorithms, and error handling

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · expected input and possible output
texts.text: expected input and possible output

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · aids to testing and how to use them
texts.text: aids to testing and how to use them

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · expected extensions or revisions
texts.text: expected extensions or revisions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Your organizational standards usually specify the order and content of the header comment block. Here is how a typical header comment block might look:
texts.text: Your organizational standards usually specify the order and content of the header comment block. Here is how a typical header comment block might look:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: PROGRAM SCAN: Program to scan a line of text for a given character PROGRAMMER: Beatrice Clarman (718) 345-6789/bc@power.com CALLING SEQUENCE: CALL SCAN(LENGTH,CHAR,NTEXT) where LENGTH is the length of the line to be scanned; CHAR is the character sought. Line of text is passed as array NTEXT. VERSION 1: written 3 November 2000 by B. Clarman REVISION 1.1: 5 December 2001 by B. Clarman to improve searching algorithm. PURPOSE: General-purpose scanning module to be used for each new line of text, no matter the length. One of several text utilities designed to add a character to a line of text, read a character, change a character, or delete a character. DATA STRUCTURES: Variable LENGTH - integer Variable CHAR - character
texts.text: PROGRAM SCAN: Program to scan a line of text for a given character PROGRAMMER: Beatrice Clarman (718) 345-6789/bc@power.com CALLING SEQUENCE: CALL SCAN(LENGTH,CHAR,NTEXT) where LENGTH is the length of the line to be scanned; CHAR is the character sought. Line of text is passed as array NTEXT. VERSION 1: written 3 November 2000 by B. Clarman REVISION 1.1: 5 December 2001 by B. Clarman to improve searching algorithm. PURPOSE: General-purpose scanning module to be used for each new line of text, no matter the length. One of several text utilities designed to add a character to a line of text, read a character, change a character, or delete a character. DATA STRUCTURES: Variable LENGTH - integer Variable CHAR - character

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Array NTEXT - character array of length LENGTH
texts.text: Array NTEXT - character array of length LENGTH

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ALGORITHM: Reads array NTEXT one character at a time; if CHAR is found, position in NTEXT returned in variable LENGTH; else variable LENGTH set to 0.
texts.text: ALGORITHM: Reads array NTEXT one character at a time; if CHAR is found, position in NTEXT returned in variable LENGTH; else variable LENGTH set to 0.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Other Program Comments. The header comment block acts as an introduction to your program, much as the introduction to a book explains its purpose. Additional comments enlighten readers as they move through your program, helping them understand how the intentions you describe in the header are implemented in the code. If the code's organization reflects a well-structured design, if the statements are formatted clearly, and if the labels, variable names, and data names are descriptive and easy to distinguish, then the necessary number of additional comments is small.That is, following simple guidelines for code format and structure allows the code to be a source of information about itself.
texts.text: Other Program Comments. The header comment block acts as an introduction to your program, much as the introduction to a book explains its purpose. Additional comments enlighten readers as they move through your program, helping them understand how the intentions you describe in the header are implemented in the code. If the code's organization reflects a well-structured design, if the statements are formatted clearly, and if the labels, variable names, and data names are descriptive and easy to distinguish, then the necessary number of additional comments is small.That is, following simple guidelines for code format and structure allows the code to be a source of information about itself.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Comments have a place even in clearly structured and well-written code.Although code clarity and structure minimize the need for other comments, additional comments are useful wherever helpful information can be added to a component. Besides providing  a  line-by-line  explanation  of  what  the  program  is  doing, the  comments  can  also break the code into phases that represent major activities. Then, each activity can be divided into yet smaller steps, each only several lines in length. Pseudocode from your program design can serve this purpose and be embedded in the code itself. Also, when code is revised, programmers should update the comments to reflect the changes. In this way, the comments build a record of revisions over time.
texts.text: Comments have a place even in clearly structured and well-written code.Although code clarity and structure minimize the need for other comments, additional comments are useful wherever helpful information can be added to a component. Besides providing  a  line-by-line  explanation  of  what  the  program  is  doing, the  comments  can  also break the code into phases that represent major activities. Then, each activity can be divided into yet smaller steps, each only several lines in length. Pseudocode from your program design can serve this purpose and be embedded in the code itself. Also, when code is revised, programmers should update the comments to reflect the changes. In this way, the comments build a record of revisions over time.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is essential that the comments reflect the actual code behavior. In addition, make sure that the comments add new information, rather than state what is already obvious from your use of good labels and variable names. For example, it is useless to write
texts.text: It is essential that the comments reflect the actual code behavior. In addition, make sure that the comments add new information, rather than state what is already obvious from your use of good labels and variable names. For example, it is useless to write

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Usually, you begin coding by moving from the design to pseudocode, which in turn provides a framework for your final code and a basis for your comments. Be sure to write the comments as you write the code, not afterward, so you capture both the design and your intention. Beware of code that is difficult to comment; the difficulty often suggests that the design should be simplified before you finish coding.
texts.text: Usually, you begin coding by moving from the design to pseudocode, which in turn provides a framework for your final code and a basis for your comments. Be sure to write the comments as you write the code, not afterward, so you capture both the design and your intention. Beware of code that is difficult to comment; the difficulty often suggests that the design should be simplified before you finish coding.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Meaningful  Variable  Names  and  Statement  Labels. Choose  names  for  your variables and statements that reflect their use or meaning.Writing
texts.text: Meaningful  Variable  Names  and  Statement  Labels. Choose  names  for  your variables and statements that reflect their use or meaning.Writing

texts.label: code
texts.orig: weekwage = (hrrate * hours) + (.5)* (hrrate) * (hours - 40.);
texts.text: weekwage = (hrrate * hours) + (.5)* (hrrate) * (hours - 40.);

texts.text: makes more sense to the reader than

texts.orig: In fact, the weekwage example is not likely to need comments at all, and you are less likely to introduce faults.
texts.text: In fact, the weekwage example is not likely to need comments at all, and you are less likely to introduce faults.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Similarly, alphabetic statement labels should tell readers something about what the labeled sections of your program do. If the labels must be numeric, then be sure they are in ascending order and clustered by related purpose.
texts.text: Similarly, alphabetic statement labels should tell readers something about what the labeled sections of your program do. If the labels must be numeric, then be sure they are in ascending order and clustered by related purpose.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Formatting to Enhance Understanding. The format of your comments can help a reader understand the goal of the code and how the goal is reached. Indentation and spacing of statements can reflect the basic control structure. Notice how unindented code like this
texts.text: Formatting to Enhance Understanding. The format of your comments can help a reader understand the goal of the code and how the goal is reached. Indentation and spacing of statements can reflect the basic control structure. Notice how unindented code like this

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition to using format to display the control structure, Weinberg (1971) recommends formatting your statements so that the comments appear on one side of the page and the statements on the other. In this way, you can cover up the comments when testing your program and thus not be misled by what may be incorrect documentation. For example, the following code (from Lee and Tepfenhart 1997) can be read without comments by looking only at the left side of the page.
texts.text: In addition to using format to display the control structure, Weinberg (1971) recommends formatting your statements so that the comments appear on one side of the page and the statements on the other. In this way, you can cover up the comments when testing your program and thus not be misled by what may be incorrect documentation. For example, the following code (from Lee and Tepfenhart 1997) can be read without comments by looking only at the left side of the page.

texts.label: code
texts.orig: void free_store_empty() { static int i = 0; if(i++ == 0) //guard against cerr //allocating memory cerr << 'Out of memory\n'; //tell user abort(); //give up }
texts.text: void free_store_empty() { static int i = 0; if(i++ == 0) //guard against cerr //allocating memory cerr << 'Out of memory\n'; //tell user abort(); //give up }

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Documenting Data. One of the  most  difficult  things  for  program  readers  to understand is the way in which data are structured and used.A data map is very useful in interpreting the code's actions, especially when a system handles many files of varying types and purposes, coupled with flags and passed parameters.This map should correspond with the data dictionary in the external documentation, so the reader can track data manipulation through the requirements and design to the code.
texts.text: Documenting Data. One of the  most  difficult  things  for  program  readers  to understand is the way in which data are structured and used.A data map is very useful in interpreting the code's actions, especially when a system handles many files of varying types and purposes, coupled with flags and passed parameters.This map should correspond with the data dictionary in the external documentation, so the reader can track data manipulation through the requirements and design to the code.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Object-oriented designs minimize or eliminate some of these problems, but sometimes this information hiding makes it even more difficult for the reader to understand exactly how a data value is changed. Thus, the internal documentation should include descriptions of the data structures and uses.
texts.text: Object-oriented designs minimize or eliminate some of these problems, but sometimes this information hiding makes it even more difficult for the reader to understand exactly how a data value is changed. Thus, the internal documentation should include descriptions of the data structures and uses.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: External Documentation
texts.text: External Documentation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Whereas internal documentation is concise and written at a level appropriate for a programmer, external documentation is intended to be read also by those who may never look at the actual code. For example, designers may review the external documentation when considering modifications or enhancements. In addition, the external documentation gives you a chance to explain things more broadly than might be reasonable within your  program's  comments. If  you  consider  the  header  comment  block  to  be  an overview or summary of your program, then the external documentation is the fullblown report. It answers the same questions-who, what, why, when, where, and howusing a system rather than a component perspective.
texts.text: Whereas internal documentation is concise and written at a level appropriate for a programmer, external documentation is intended to be read also by those who may never look at the actual code. For example, designers may review the external documentation when considering modifications or enhancements. In addition, the external documentation gives you a chance to explain things more broadly than might be reasonable within your  program's  comments. If  you  consider  the  header  comment  block  to  be  an overview or summary of your program, then the external documentation is the fullblown report. It answers the same questions-who, what, why, when, where, and howusing a system rather than a component perspective.

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Section 7.4
texts.text: Section 7.4

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: The Programming Process
texts.text: The Programming Process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Because a software system is built from interrelated components, the external documentation often includes an overview of the system's components or of several groupings of components (the user-interface components, the database e.g., management components,the ground-speed-calculation components).Diagrams,accompanied by narrative describing each component, show how data are shared and used by one or more components; in general, the overview describes how information is passed from one component to another. Object classes and their inheritance hierarchy are explained here, as are reasons for defining special types or categories of data.
texts.text: Because a software system is built from interrelated components, the external documentation often includes an overview of the system's components or of several groupings of components (the user-interface components, the database e.g., management components,the ground-speed-calculation components).Diagrams,accompanied by narrative describing each component, show how data are shared and used by one or more components; in general, the overview describes how information is passed from one component to another. Object classes and their inheritance hierarchy are explained here, as are reasons for defining special types or categories of data.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: External component documentation is part of the overall system documentation. At the time the component is written, much of the rationale for the component's structure and flow have already been detailed in the design documents. In a sense, the design is the skeleton of the external documentation, and the flesh is supplied by narrative discussing the code component's particulars.
texts.text: External component documentation is part of the overall system documentation. At the time the component is written, much of the rationale for the component's structure and flow have already been detailed in the design documents. In a sense, the design is the skeleton of the external documentation, and the flesh is supplied by narrative discussing the code component's particulars.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Describing the Problem. In the first section of the code's documentation, you should explain what problem is being addressed by the component. This section sets the stage for describing what options were considered for solutions and why a particular solution was chosen. The problem description is not a repeat of the requirements documentation; rather, it is a general discussion of the setting, explaining when the component is invoked and why it is needed.
texts.text: Describing the Problem. In the first section of the code's documentation, you should explain what problem is being addressed by the component. This section sets the stage for describing what options were considered for solutions and why a particular solution was chosen. The problem description is not a repeat of the requirements documentation; rather, it is a general discussion of the setting, explaining when the component is invoked and why it is needed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Describing the Algorithms. Once you make clear why the component exists, you should address the choice of algorithms. You should explain each algorithm used by the component, including formulas, boundary or special conditions, and even its derivation or reference to the book or paper from which it is derived.
texts.text: Describing the Algorithms. Once you make clear why the component exists, you should address the choice of algorithms. You should explain each algorithm used by the component, including formulas, boundary or special conditions, and even its derivation or reference to the book or paper from which it is derived.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If an algorithm deals with special cases, be sure to discuss each one and explain how it is handled. If certain cases are not handled because they are not expected to be encountered, explain  your  rationale  and  describe  any  related  error  handling  in  the code. For example, an algorithm may include a formula where one variable is divided by another.The documentation should address cases where the denominator might be zero, pointing out when this situation might occur and how it is handled by the code.
texts.text: If an algorithm deals with special cases, be sure to discuss each one and explain how it is handled. If certain cases are not handled because they are not expected to be encountered, explain  your  rationale  and  describe  any  related  error  handling  in  the code. For example, an algorithm may include a formula where one variable is divided by another.The documentation should address cases where the denominator might be zero, pointing out when this situation might occur and how it is handled by the code.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Describing the Data. In the external documentation, the users or programmers should be able to view the data flow at the component level. Data flow diagrams should be accompanied by relevant data dictionary references. For object-oriented components, the  overview  of  objects  and  classes  should  explain  the  general  interaction  of objects.
texts.text: Describing the Data. In the external documentation, the users or programmers should be able to view the data flow at the component level. Data flow diagrams should be accompanied by relevant data dictionary references. For object-oriented components, the  overview  of  objects  and  classes  should  explain  the  general  interaction  of objects.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 7.4 THE PROGRAMMING PROCESS
texts.text: 7.4 THE PROGRAMMING PROCESS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This chapter discusses guidelines, standards, and documentation. What kind of process do programmers follow to ensure that the code they produce is of high quality? Until recently, the programming process was not the focus of much research. As Whittaker and Atkin (2002) point out, software engineering researchers often assume that, given a good design, any programmer can translate the design into solid code. But good code is the result not only of a good design but also of skill, experience, and artfulness derived

Until recently, the programming process was not the focus of much research. As Whittaker and Atkin (2002) point out, software engineering researchers often assume that, given a good design, any programmer can translate the design into solid code. But good code is the result not only of a good design but also of skill, experience, and artfulness derived

texts.orig: Chapter 7
texts.text: Chapter 7

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Writing the Programs
texts.text: Writing the Programs

texts.label: text
texts.orig: from imagination and good problem solving. To understand what processes support good programming practices, we begin by examining how to solve problems.
texts.text: from imagination and good problem solving. To understand what processes support good programming practices, we begin by examining how to solve problems.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Programming as Problem Solving
texts.text: Programming as Problem Solving

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polyà (1957) wrote the classic book on how we solve problems. He points out that finding a good solution involves four distinct stages:
texts.text: Polyà (1957) wrote the classic book on how we solve problems. He points out that finding a good solution involves four distinct stages:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Understanding the problem
texts.text: Understanding the problem

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Devising a plan
texts.text: Devising a plan

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. Carrying out the plan
texts.text: Carrying out the plan

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. Looking back
texts.text: Looking back

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To understand the problem, we analyze each of its elements. What is known? What is not known? What is possible to know? What is impossible to know? These questions address both data and relationships. Just as a good systems analyst frames a problem in terms of the system boundary, identifying what is inside the boundary and what is outside, a good problem solver must understand the conditions under which the problem must be solved.Then, once we can define the conditions, we ask if the conditions can be satisfied.
texts.text: To understand the problem, we analyze each of its elements. What is known? What is not known? What is possible to know? What is impossible to know? These questions address both data and relationships. Just as a good systems analyst frames a problem in terms of the system boundary, identifying what is inside the boundary and what is outside, a good problem solver must understand the conditions under which the problem must be solved.Then, once we can define the conditions, we ask if the conditions can be satisfied.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sometimes it is helpful to draw a picture to enhance understanding. Programmers often do this by drawing a flow chart or diagram to show how a dynamically allocated data structure changes shape as various operations are performed on it. Such depiction helps us to identify various parts of the condition, and perhaps even to decompose the problem into smaller, more tractable problems. In a way, this reframing to gain understanding is a micro-design activity: the program design is viewed carefully and reorganized in pieces that are easier to address than the original framing of the design.
texts.text: Sometimes it is helpful to draw a picture to enhance understanding. Programmers often do this by drawing a flow chart or diagram to show how a dynamically allocated data structure changes shape as various operations are performed on it. Such depiction helps us to identify various parts of the condition, and perhaps even to decompose the problem into smaller, more tractable problems. In a way, this reframing to gain understanding is a micro-design activity: the program design is viewed carefully and reorganized in pieces that are easier to address than the original framing of the design.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Next, a plan is devised to determine how a solution can be derived from what is known  about  the  problem. What  are  the  connections  between  the  data  and  the unknowns? If no connection is immediately clear, then are there ways in which this problem is similar to other, better-known problems? It is here that we try to think of the current problem in terms of patterns: Is there a pattern or set of patterns that we can adapt to fit our problem?
texts.text: Next, a plan is devised to determine how a solution can be derived from what is known  about  the  problem. What  are  the  connections  between  the  data  and  the unknowns? If no connection is immediately clear, then are there ways in which this problem is similar to other, better-known problems? It is here that we try to think of the current problem in terms of patterns: Is there a pattern or set of patterns that we can adapt to fit our problem?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We can try several techniques to find the right plan of attack:
texts.text: We can try several techniques to find the right plan of attack:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Identifying a related problem: Are there data, algorithms, libraries, or methods that we can use to attack our problem?
texts.text: Identifying a related problem: Are there data, algorithms, libraries, or methods that we can use to attack our problem?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Restating the problem: What are the key definitions? Can the problem be generalized or made more specific to make it more tractable? Can simplifying assumptions be made?
texts.text: Restating the problem: What are the key definitions? Can the problem be generalized or made more specific to make it more tractable? Can simplifying assumptions be made?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Decomposing the problem: What are the essential elements of the problem? Can the data be partitioned into categories, so that each category can be processed separately?
texts.text: Decomposing the problem: What are the essential elements of the problem? Can the data be partitioned into categories, so that each category can be processed separately?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Perkins  (2001)  suggests  ways  to  brainstorm  and  arrive  at  what  he  calls  the 'Eureka' effect: recognizing the best solution. He wants us to rove through many different possibilities, stretching our minds to think even of implausible scenarios.We can also look for subtle clues that signal anomalies-reasons that a possible solution will

He wants us to rove through many different possibilities, stretching our minds to think even of implausible scenarios.We can also look for subtle clues that signal anomalies-reasons that a possible solution will

texts.text: Section 7.4

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: The Programming Process
texts.text: The Programming Process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: not work. In addition to reframing the problem, Perkins suggests 'decentering' from current fixations by examining every assumption and attitude. He tells us to think of creativity as a sophisticated form of questioning everything; we use our creativity to question proposed solutions and to find a better solution to our problem.
texts.text: not work. In addition to reframing the problem, Perkins suggests 'decentering' from current fixations by examining every assumption and attitude. He tells us to think of creativity as a sophisticated form of questioning everything; we use our creativity to question proposed solutions and to find a better solution to our problem.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Once the plan of attack is chosen, Polyà tells us to carry it out. Step by step, we must verify that the solution-in our case our program-is correct. If necessary, we can use some of the techniques presented in Chapters 8 and 9 to evaluate each step, making sure that each logical statement follows from previous steps.When the solution is done, we look back and examine it, revisiting each part of the argument.At the same time, we evaluate the solution for its applicability to other situations. Is the solution reusable? Can it be used to solve a different problem? Should it become the basis for a pattern?
texts.text: Once the plan of attack is chosen, Polyà tells us to carry it out. Step by step, we must verify that the solution-in our case our program-is correct. If necessary, we can use some of the techniques presented in Chapters 8 and 9 to evaluate each step, making sure that each logical statement follows from previous steps.When the solution is done, we look back and examine it, revisiting each part of the argument.At the same time, we evaluate the solution for its applicability to other situations. Is the solution reusable? Can it be used to solve a different problem? Should it become the basis for a pattern?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Any programming process that encourages these steps can enhance the speed at which we find a good solution. Libraries of programs, good documentation, and automated tools support a good process.As we will see in later chapters, reuse repositories, change management, and post-mortems also improve the process, particularly by analyzing our successes and failures and by encoding in our products and processes the recommendations from our analyses, so that programming is easier the next time around.
texts.text: Any programming process that encourages these steps can enhance the speed at which we find a good solution. Libraries of programs, good documentation, and automated tools support a good process.As we will see in later chapters, reuse repositories, change management, and post-mortems also improve the process, particularly by analyzing our successes and failures and by encoding in our products and processes the recommendations from our analyses, so that programming is easier the next time around.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Extreme Programming
texts.text: Extreme Programming

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Chapter 2, we learned about agile methods and the related philosophy of programming, called extreme programming. In extreme programming, there are two types of participants: customers and programmers. Customers represent the users of the eventual system. In this role, they perform the following activities:
texts.text: In Chapter 2, we learned about agile methods and the related philosophy of programming, called extreme programming. In extreme programming, there are two types of participants: customers and programmers. Customers represent the users of the eventual system. In this role, they perform the following activities:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Define  the  features  that  the  programmers  will  implement, using  stories  to describe the way the system will work
texts.text: Define  the  features  that  the  programmers  will  implement, using  stories  to describe the way the system will work

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Describe detailed tests that they will run when the software is ready, to verify that the stories were implemented properly
texts.text: Describe detailed tests that they will run when the software is ready, to verify that the stories were implemented properly

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Assign priorities to the stories and their tests
texts.text: Assign priorities to the stories and their tests

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In turn, the programmers write code to implement the stories, doing so in the order specified by the customers' priorities. To assist the customers, the programmers must estimate how long it will take to code up each story, so that the customers can plan the acceptance tests. The planning occurs in two-week increments, so that trust builds up between customers and programmers. Martin (2000) points out that extreme programming involves more than just this dialog between programmers and customers; there are also dialogs between programmers as they pair program: sitting side by side at a workstation and collaborating on the construction of a single program.
texts.text: In turn, the programmers write code to implement the stories, doing so in the order specified by the customers' priorities. To assist the customers, the programmers must estimate how long it will take to code up each story, so that the customers can plan the acceptance tests. The planning occurs in two-week increments, so that trust builds up between customers and programmers. Martin (2000) points out that extreme programming involves more than just this dialog between programmers and customers; there are also dialogs between programmers as they pair program: sitting side by side at a workstation and collaborating on the construction of a single program.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Pair Programming
texts.text: Pair Programming

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The concept of pair programming is one of the more radical of the agile method techniques, so we examine it in more depth in this chapter. In pair programming, a pair of programmers uses a single keyboard, display, and mouse. Each member of the pair assumes a very specific role. One person is the driver or pilot, controlling the computer and actually writing the code. The other person is the navigator, reviewing the driver's code and providing feedback.The two team members exchange roles periodically.
texts.text: The concept of pair programming is one of the more radical of the agile method techniques, so we examine it in more depth in this chapter. In pair programming, a pair of programmers uses a single keyboard, display, and mouse. Each member of the pair assumes a very specific role. One person is the driver or pilot, controlling the computer and actually writing the code. The other person is the navigator, reviewing the driver's code and providing feedback.The two team members exchange roles periodically.
